result:
Done. The spec is fully implemented, the project builds cleanly, and I verified the behavior end-to-end — all 25 behavioral checks (including round-trip cases) pass.

The source turned out to be a Calor DSL file (`CsvRow.calr`), not C#, so I first learned the language from the toolchain repo's syntax reference and test fixtures, then added the three functions to the existing `CsvRow` module alongside `NeedsQuoting` (which is untouched):

- **`EscapeField`** — calls `NeedsQuoting`; if quoting is needed, doubles internal quotes with `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")` and wraps in quotes, otherwise returns the field verbatim.
- **`JoinRow`** — walks the list with a while loop, escaping each field and joining with commas; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** — a character-scanning parser: quoted fields consume until an un-doubled quote (doubled quotes become one literal quote, commas/CR/LF inside are content), unquoted fields end at commas, and a final `push` after the loop handles both the empty line (→ one empty field) and trailing commas (→ trailing empty field).

Three build cycles were needed, fixing two Calor-specific issues along the way: element access uses brace syntax (`fields{i}`, not `fields[i]`), an empty `§LIST` block needs an explicit `§/LIST{name}` closer, and `SplitRow` had to declare `§E{mut}` because the effects checker counts `§PUSH` on the (locally-created) list as a mutation effect — the function is still externally pure per the spec.

To verify behavior rather than just compilation, I built a throwaway console harness in a temp directory (outside the project, deleted afterward) that loaded the compiled `Src.dll` and exercised all four functions: escaping edge cases (lone quote, CRLF, empty field), join edge cases (empty list, `["",""]` → `","`), split edge cases (quoted empty `""`, embedded CRLF, leading/trailing commas), and `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` round-trips on six adversarial field lists. Everything passed.
